content.text: What Remediation Tasks Do

A remediation task is an ARM resource (Microsoft.PolicyInsights/remediations) that drives the DeployIfNotExists (DINE) or Modify policy effects against existing non-compliant resources. When a DINE or Modify policy is assigned, it automatically applies to resources created after the assignment, but it does not retroactively fix resources that were already non-compliant before the assignment was created. A remediation task fills that gap — it scans for non-compliant resources, deploys the companion resources or modifies the properties using the policy assignment's managed identity, and tracks the deployment outcomes per resource.

attrs.alt: Remediation task lifecycle: discovery → managed identity deployment → compliance state update

content.text: Only DINE and Modify policies support remediation tasks. Audit, AuditIfNotExists, Deny, DenyAction, Append, and Disabled policies do not have a deployment mechanism and therefore do not support remediation tasks.
Creating a Remediation Task

# Basic remediation task for a DINE assignment at subscription scope
az policy remediation create \
  --name remediate-vm-law-agent \
  --subscription $SUB_ID \
  --policy-assignment /subscriptions/$SUB_ID/providers/Microsoft.Authorization/policyAssignments/deploy-law-agent

# Remediation for a DINE policy within an initiative assignment
# Must specify --definition-reference-id to target the specific DINE policy
az policy remediation create \
  --name remediate-kv-diag \
  --subscription $SUB_ID \
  --policy-assignment /providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/mg-corp/providers/Microsoft.Authorization/policyAssignments/corp-baseline \
  --definition-reference-id deployKvDiag

# Remediation at management group scope
az policy remediation create \
  --name remediate-vm-agent-mg \
  --management-group mg-corp \
  --policy-assignment /providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/mg-corp/providers/Microsoft.Authorization/policyAssignments/deploy-law-agent \
  --resource-discovery-mode ReEvaluateCompliance

# Remediation scoped to a specific resource group
az policy remediation create \
  --name remediate-vm-agent-rg \
  --resource-group rg-prod \
  --policy-assignment /subscriptions/$SUB_ID/providers/Microsoft.Authorization/policyAssignments/deploy-law-agent \
  --resource-discovery-mode ExistingNonCompliant

# Remediation for a single resource only
az policy remediation create \
  --name remediate-single-vm \
  --subscription $SUB_ID \
  --policy-assignment /subscriptions/$SUB_ID/providers/Microsoft.Authorization/policyAssignments/deploy-law-agent \
  --resource-ids /subscriptions/$SUB_ID/resourceGroups/rg-prod/providers/Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/vm-web-01
resourceDiscoveryMode: ExistingNonCompliant vs ReEvaluateCompliance

The resourceDiscoveryMode parameter controls how the remediation task discovers which resources to remediate. ExistingNonCompliant uses the last known compliance state from the PolicyInsights store — faster because it does not re-scan, but may miss resources whose state has changed since the last scan or include resources that are already compliant based on recent changes. ReEvaluateCompliance runs a fresh compliance scan before generating the remediation list — slower but more accurate.
Mode
What it does
When to use
ExistingNonCompliant (default)
Reads last-known NonCompliant state from PolicyInsights; remediates those resources immediately
Most scenarios — faster; accurate if recent scan has run
ReEvaluateCompliance
Triggers a full compliance scan first, then remediates only resources that are confirmed NonCompliant
After updating a policy definition or assignment parameters; after a large resource group change; when confident the PolicyInsights state is stale
Monitoring Remediation Task Progress

# Check remediation task status
az policy remediation show \
  --name remediate-vm-law-agent \
  --subscription $SUB_ID \
  --query '{
    Status:provisioningState,
    Total:deploymentSummary.totalDeployments,
    Success:deploymentSummary.successfulDeployments,
    Failed:deploymentSummary.failedDeployments,
    Skipped:deploymentSummary.skippedDeployments
  }' -o json

# List all remediation deployments (per-resource status)
az policy remediation deployment list \
  --name remediate-vm-law-agent \
  --subscription $SUB_ID \
  --query '[].{Resource:resourceId,Status:provisioningState,Error:error.message}' \
  -o table

# Find failed deployments (useful for debugging)
az policy remediation deployment list \
  --name remediate-vm-law-agent \
  --subscription $SUB_ID \
  --query "[?provisioningState=='Failed'].{Resource:resourceId,Error:error.message,Code:error.code}" \
  -o table

# List all remediation tasks in a subscription
az policy remediation list \
  --subscription $SUB_ID \
  --query '[].{Name:name,Policy:policyAssignmentId,Status:provisioningState,Total:deploymentSummary.totalDeployments}' \
  -o table

# Cancel an in-progress remediation task
az policy remediation cancel \
  --name remediate-vm-law-agent \
  --subscription $SUB_ID
Common Remediation Failure Causes
Failure Cause
Error Code or Message
Resolution
Managed identity missing required RBAC role
AuthorizationFailed or ResourceNotFound during deployment
Grant the assignment's managed identity the required role (usually Contributor at the remediation scope)
Deployment template has invalid ARM expression
InvalidTemplate or TemplateParseError
Fix the policy definition's deployment template; re-assign and re-remediate
Resource in a locked resource group
ScopeLocked — resource is locked
Remove the CanNotDelete or ReadOnly lock; then re-remediate
VM is deallocated — extension cannot be installed
VMStoppedError or OperationNotAllowed
Start the VM first; extension deployment requires the VM to be running
Resource already has a different companion (conflict)
ResourceAlreadyExists with different properties
Update the existenceCondition to be specific enough that the existing companion matches
ReEvaluateCompliance scan takes too long
Task stays in Evaluating state for hours
Use ExistingNonCompliant mode for large scopes; run trigger-scan separately first
Remediating at Scale with Parallel Tasks

A single remediation task can remediate up to 50,000 resources. For large environments, you may want to break remediation into subscription-scoped tasks running in parallel rather than one management-group-scoped task. Subscription-scoped tasks complete faster because the managed identity's deployments are limited to one subscription at a time, and multiple tasks can run concurrently across subscriptions.

# Parallel remediation: one task per subscription under mg-corp
POLICY_ASSIGNMENT_ID='/providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/mg-corp/providers/Microsoft.Authorization/policyAssignments/deploy-law-agent'

az account management-group entities list \
  --query "[?type=='/subscriptions'].name" \
  -o tsv | while read SUB; do
  echo "Creating remediation for subscription: $SUB"
  az policy remediation create \
    --name "remediate-law-agent-${SUB}" \
    --subscription "$SUB" \
    --policy-assignment $POLICY_ASSIGNMENT_ID \
    --resource-discovery-mode ExistingNonCompliant &
done
wait
echo 'All remediation tasks submitted'

# Monitor progress across all tasks
az account management-group entities list \
  --query "[?type=='/subscriptions'].name" \
  -o tsv | while read SUB; do
  STATUS=$(az policy remediation show \
    --name "remediate-law-agent-${SUB}" \
    --subscription "$SUB" \
    --query 'provisioningState' -o tsv 2>/dev/null || echo 'NotFound')
  echo "$SUB: $STATUS"
done

# Summarize success/failure counts across all remediations
az account management-group entities list \
  --query "[?type=='/subscriptions'].name" \
  -o tsv | while read SUB; do
  az policy remediation show \
    --name "remediate-law-agent-${SUB}" \
    --subscription "$SUB" \
    --query 'deploymentSummary' -o json 2>/dev/null
done
Terraform: Remediation Task as Part of Deployment

In Terraform, a remediation task is represented by the azurerm_resource_policy_remediation or azurerm_management_group_policy_remediation resource (depending on scope). Including a remediation task in Terraform ensures that historical resources are remediated automatically as part of the same apply that creates the policy assignment. The task should depend on both the assignment and the role assignment so the managed identity has the required permissions before the remediation begins.
Resource Graph: Monitoring Remediation at Scale

// All remediation tasks across subscriptions with their status
PolicyResources
| where type =~ 'Microsoft.PolicyInsights/remediations'
| project
    name            = name,
    scope           = resourceGroup,
    sub             = subscriptionId,
    status          = tostring(properties.provisioningState),
    totalDeploys    = toint(properties.deploymentSummary.totalDeployments),
    successDeploys  = toint(properties.deploymentSummary.successfulDeployments),
    failedDeploys   = toint(properties.deploymentSummary.failedDeployments),
    policyAssign    = tostring(properties.policyAssignmentId)
| order by status asc, failedDeploys desc

// Remediations that have failed deployments (need investigation)
PolicyResources
| where type =~ 'Microsoft.PolicyInsights/remediations'
| where toint(properties.deploymentSummary.failedDeployments) > 0
| project
    name          = name,
    sub           = subscriptionId,
    status        = tostring(properties.provisioningState),
    failed        = toint(properties.deploymentSummary.failedDeployments),
    total         = toint(properties.deploymentSummary.totalDeployments)
| order by failed desc

// Resources that are STILL NonCompliant after a remediation task ran
// (deployment failed or compliance not yet updated)
PolicyResources
| where type =~ 'Microsoft.PolicyInsights/PolicyStates'
| where properties.complianceState == 'NonCompliant'
| where properties.policyDefinitionAction == 'deployifnotexists'
| where properties.policyAssignmentName == 'deploy-law-agent'
| project
    resourceId = tostring(properties.resourceId),
    sub        = subscriptionId,
    rg         = tostring(properties.resourceGroup)
| order by sub asc, rg asc

// Track remediation success rate by assignment
PolicyResources
| where type =~ 'Microsoft.PolicyInsights/remediations'
| summarize
    TotalDeploys   = sum(toint(properties.deploymentSummary.totalDeployments)),
    SuccessDeploys = sum(toint(properties.deploymentSummary.successfulDeployments)),
    FailedDeploys  = sum(toint(properties.deploymentSummary.failedDeployments))
    by assignment = tostring(properties.policyAssignmentId)
| extend SuccessRate = round(100.0 * SuccessDeploys / TotalDeploys, 1)
| order by SuccessRate asc
Pre-Remediation Checklist

Running a remediation task on a large MG scope without preparation can lead to failed deployments, alert storms, or unexpected resource changes. Following a pre-remediation checklist reduces the blast radius of failures and ensures the managed identity has the required permissions before deployments begin.
Check
Why
How
Managed identity has required role
AuthorizationFailed errors on every deployment without this
az role assignment list --assignee $IDENTITY_OID
Tested policy in sandbox first
Template errors only appear when the identity attempts the actual deployment
Assign at test sub → create test resource → verify companion appears
existenceCondition is specific enough
Overly broad condition causes false Compliant — remediation skips already-wrong companions
Review existenceCondition; compare with actual companion properties
Resource lock audit
ScopeLocked errors for resources in locked resource groups
az lock list --scope /subscriptions/$SUB_ID
VM running state verified (for VM extensions)
Extension installation fails on deallocated VMs
az vm list --query "[?powerState!='VM running'].{Name:name,State:powerState}"
Remediation scope matches assignment scope
Remediation at broader scope than assignment does nothing
Verify --management-group or --subscription matches assignment scope
Handling Remediation at Large Scale: Batching and Rate Limiting

Azure Policy remediation tasks internally batch deployments and respect ARM throttling limits. Each deployment is a separate ARM write operation against the resource group. ARM's per-resource-group write limit is 1200 requests per hour. For large remediations across hundreds of VMs in the same resource group, the task automatically paces deployments to avoid hitting the limit. You cannot tune this pacing — it is managed by the platform.

# Check if ARM throttling is causing remediation delays
# Look for HTTP 429 (TooManyRequests) in the remediation deployment list
az policy remediation deployment list \
  --name remediate-vm-law-agent \
  --subscription $SUB_ID \
  --query "[?error.code=='TooManyRequests'].{Resource:resourceId,Error:error.message}" \
  -o table

# If throttled: split into smaller batches by resource group
az policy remediation create \
  --name remediate-rg-prod-01 \
  --subscription $SUB_ID \
  --policy-assignment /subscriptions/$SUB_ID/providers/Microsoft.Authorization/policyAssignments/deploy-law-agent \
  --resource-group rg-prod-01

az policy remediation create \
  --name remediate-rg-prod-02 \
  --subscription $SUB_ID \
  --policy-assignment /subscriptions/$SUB_ID/providers/Microsoft.Authorization/policyAssignments/deploy-law-agent \
  --resource-group rg-prod-02

# Check Resource Provider throttle limits for your subscription
az rest \
  --method get \
  --uri "https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/$SUB_ID/providers/Microsoft.Compute?api-version=2022-09-01" \
  --query 'resourceTypes[?resourceType==`virtualMachines/extensions`].resourceType'

# After remediation: verify compliance state updated
az policy state trigger-scan --subscription $SUB_ID
echo 'Waiting for scan results...'
sleep 120
az policy state summarize \
  --subscription $SUB_ID \
  --filter "policyAssignmentName eq 'deploy-law-agent'" \
  --query 'results.resourceDetails'
Automating Remediation with Azure DevOps Pipelines

Integrating remediation task creation into a CI/CD pipeline ensures that every policy assignment change is followed immediately by a remediation pass against existing resources. The pattern: deploy Terraform (assignment + role assignment) → wait for managed identity role propagation (90–120 seconds) → trigger remediation via CLI → poll for completion. The pipeline can fail the job if the remediation task ends in Failed or Partial state with zero successful deployments.

# azure-pipelines.yml — policy assignment + remediation pipeline
trigger:
  branches:
    include: [main]
  paths:
    include: [terraform/policy/**]

pool:
  vmImage: ubuntu-latest

variables:
  MANAGEMENT_GROUP_ID: mg-corp
  POLICY_ASSIGNMENT_NAME: deploy-law-agent

stages:
- stage: Deploy
  jobs:
  - job: TerraformApply
    steps:
    - task: TerraformTaskV4@4
      displayName: Terraform Apply (policy + assignment + role)
      inputs:
        command: apply
        workingDirectory: terraform/policy
        environmentServiceNameARM: my-azure-service-connection

    - task: AzureCLI@2
      displayName: Wait for MI role propagation
      inputs:
        azureSubscription: my-azure-service-connection
        scriptType: bash
        scriptLocation: inlineScript
        inlineScript: |
          echo 'Waiting 120s for role assignment propagation...'
          sleep 120

    - task: AzureCLI@2
      displayName: Create remediation task
      inputs:
        azureSubscription: my-azure-service-connection
        scriptType: bash
        scriptLocation: inlineScript
        inlineScript: |
          TASK_NAME="remediate-$(date +%Y%m%d%H%M)"

          az policy remediation create \
            --name $TASK_NAME \
            --management-group $MANAGEMENT_GROUP_ID \
            --policy-assignment /providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/$MANAGEMENT_GROUP_ID/providers/Microsoft.Authorization/policyAssignments/$POLICY_ASSIGNMENT_NAME \
            --resource-discovery-mode ReEvaluateCompliance

          echo "##vso[task.setvariable variable=TASK_NAME]$TASK_NAME"

    - task: AzureCLI@2
      displayName: Poll remediation until complete
      inputs:
        azureSubscription: my-azure-service-connection
        scriptType: bash
        scriptLocation: inlineScript
        inlineScript: |
          for i in $(seq 1 30); do
            STATUS=$(az policy remediation show \
              --name $TASK_NAME \
              --management-group $MANAGEMENT_GROUP_ID \
              --query 'provisioningState' -o tsv)
            echo "Attempt $i: status=$STATUS"
            if [[ "$STATUS" == 'Succeeded' || "$STATUS" == 'Canceled' ]]; then
              break
            fi
            if [[ "$STATUS" == 'Failed' ]]; then
              echo '##vso[task.logissue type=error]Remediation task failed'
              exit 1
            fi
            sleep 60
          done

          # Check final deployment summary
          az policy remediation show \
            --name $TASK_NAME \
            --management-group $MANAGEMENT_GROUP_ID \
            --query 'deploymentSummary' -o json
Summary

Remediation tasks apply DINE or Modify policy effects to resources that were non-compliant before the policy was assigned. Create a task with az policy remediation create, specifying the policy assignment and optionally a policyDefinitionReferenceId when targeting a policy within an initiative. Choose ExistingNonCompliant for speed or ReEvaluateCompliance for accuracy. Monitor progress via deploymentSummary and deployment list to identify and address failures. For large MG scopes, use parallel subscription-scoped tasks to improve throughput.
